Learning Objectives

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to:

  • 01Communicate the meaning of the term geographic representation.
  • 02Discriminate between discrete object and continuous surface representation and their data models.
  • 03Describe the vector data model.
  • 04Identify topological relationships among vector features.
  • 05Describe the raster data model.
  • 06Describe the advantages and disadvantages of the vector and raster models.
The big idea

Geographic representation

GIS data are a simplified view of the real world: we stand in for entities and phenomena with spatial objects plus attributes — an “entity–object correspondence.”

Representation is an abstraction — and it’s subjective.
Because of computational limits and the need to simplify for analysis, we store only a subset of “essential characteristics.” No single abstraction is universally best.

We typically organize data into thematic layers — roads, soils, elevation, and so on.

Locating things, part 1

Cartesian coordinate systems (2D, 3D)

A coordinate system built on 2–3 orthogonal (right-angle) axes — x, y, and sometimes z (elevation) — with an origin at (0, 0, 0). Position is the distance in linear units (meters, feet) from the origin along each axis.

Group brainstorm
Practice describing coordinates using Figure 2-5.

I’ll point to a location on Figure 2-5 above and call on someone to give me its coordinate — read off the (X, Y) pair relative to the origin.

Locating things, part 2

Spherical coordinate systems

Coordinates specified on a sphere using angles — most commonly latitude (φ) and longitude (λ).

  • φLatitude (“lat lies flat”) — the angle north/south of the equator. North runs 0 to +90, South 0 to −90.
  • λLongitude — the angle east/west of a prime meridian. East runs 0 to +180, West 0 to −180.

Often recorded as DMS (degrees–minutes–seconds), e.g. 38° 53′ 23″ N, 77° 00′ 32″ W, or as DD (decimal degrees), e.g. 38.889722°, −77.008889°. You’ll usually see and use DD in the GIS.

It’s easy to convert DMS ↔ DD.
DMS → DD: degrees + minutes/60 + seconds/3600, then make it negative for S or W.
DD → DMS: degrees = whole number; minutes = decimal part × 60 (whole number); seconds = leftover decimal × 60.
Group brainstorm · everyone solves
Convert (49° 14′ 46.6512″ N, 123° 6′ 58.4136″ W) into decimal degrees.

Work it out on your own, then raise your hand when you’re ready — be ready to explain how you got there.

Latitude 49° 14′ 46.6512″ N = 49 + 14/60 + 46.6512/3600 = 49 + 0.2333 + 0.0130 = 49.2459
Longitude 123° 6′ 58.4136″ W = 123 + 6/60 + 58.4136/3600 = 123 + 0.1000 + 0.0162 = 123.1162 → −123.116 (W is negative)
Answer (49.2459, −123.116)

Going the other way: DD → DMS

The reverse just peels off each piece in turn. Try converting 24.93547° to DMS on your own, then check yourself.

Worked example · DD → DMS Degrees = whole number = 24
Minutes = decimal part × 60 = 0.93547 × 60 = 56.128… → whole minutes = 56
Seconds = leftover decimal × 60 = 0.128 × 60 = 7.692
So 24.93547° = 24° 56′ 7.692″

Try your own converter

DMS → DD
Decimal degrees: —
DD → DMS
DMS: —
The “what”

Attribute data and types

Attribute data describe the non-spatial characteristics of features. They’re usually stored in tables where rows are features and columns are attributes (fields) — here, each fire hydrant is a feature with fields like flanges, color, and rating.

Attribute values come in three measurement types:

TypeDefinitionExample
NominalDescriptive categories with no implied order (can include media like images or audio).Land use: urban, agriculture, undeveloped.
OrdinalValues imply a rank order, but not meaningful numeric differences.Erosion class 1–10; size: small, medium, large.
Interval / ratioNumeric values where both order and the size of differences are meaningful.Area, height, depth, property value, population.

Spatial data models

There are two common ways to think about geography: discrete objects (things with boundaries) versus continuous fields (a value everywhere).

RepresentationDefinitionExample
Discrete objectPhenomena treated as countable entities with distinct boundaries.Buildings, parcels, road centerlines, city boundaries.
Continuous fieldPhenomena treated as varying continuously across space.Elevation, rainfall, air-pollution concentration.

That choice maps onto the two main data models: vector (great for discrete objects) and raster (great for continuous fields).

Here’s the same feature — Crater Lake — in both models in ArcGIS Pro. The vector data captures just the lake’s boundary; the raster captures elevation across the lake and surrounding terrain.

Vector spatial data models

A vector model uses sets of coordinates + attributes to define discrete objects. Three basic object types: points, lines, and polygons.

Group brainstorm
Geographic representation in Fairfax: what would you map as points, lines, or polygons?
FeaturePoint / Line / PolygonWhy?

Vector models preserve topology

Topology is a set of rules describing the spatial relationships between neighboring points, lines, and polygons. It doesn’t change when shapes are bent or stretched, and it lets the GIS answer questions like “What streets connect?”, “What county is next to what?”, and “What restaurant am I closest to?”

Early GIS used the “spaghetti” model

In the spaghetti model, lines are captured individually and the dataset is unstructured — like strands of spaghetti where crossings don’t necessarily create intersections. (Yes, like cooking spaghetti.)

  • No enforced connectivity — networks aren’t possible.
  • Polygon problems — which side is inside vs. outside?
  • Slow data access — no connecting nodes means data is redundantly stored.
  • Severely limits spatial analysis — the computer doesn’t understand how features relate.

Modern GIS uses topological vector models

Topological models enforce strict connectivity and record connectivity and adjacency using arcs and nodes — storing coordinates and relationship tables.

So the GIS can reliably answer: “What lines connect to line 12?” · “Which polygon is adjacent to A across edge 52?” · “Does polygon A’s boundary fully close?”

Raster spatial data models

A raster represents space as a regular grid of cells, with one value stored per cell — ideal for continuous fields.

The value in each cell can be different kinds of data:

Resolution

Raster resolution is the ground distance represented by each cell — the cell size that sets how much detail the raster can show. Smaller cells mean more cells and finer detail (and bigger files).

Cell assignment is complicated

When the ground inside a cell is mixed, the GIS still has to pick one value. How to choose? “Winner takes all” (the dominant class) or a “preference for” rule (favor a class that matters more).

You can convert raster ↔ vector

Vector vs. raster: strengths and trade-offs

Neither model is “better” — each wins for different jobs. Predict where each has the edge, then read across.

CharacteristicRasterVector
Data structureUsually simple.Usually complex.
StorageLarger for most data sets (without compression).Smaller for most data sets.
Coordinate conversionMay be slow on large grids; can require resampling.Simple.
AnalysisEasy for continuous data and many layer combinations.Preferred for network analysis; many spatial operations are more complex.
Spatial precisionLimited by cell size.Limited only by positional measurement.
AccessibilityEasy to modify or program (simple structure).Often complex.
Display & outputGood for images; discrete features can show “stair-step” edges.Map-like with smooth curves; poor for images.

Key takeaways

1
Every GIS representation is an abstraction — we keep only the characteristics that matter for the task.
2
Location is given by coordinates: Cartesian (linear units, x/y/z) or spherical (angular lat/long, in DMS or DD).
3
Attributes are the “what,” measured as nominal, ordinal, or interval/ratio.
4
Discrete objects → vector (points/lines/polygons); continuous fields → raster (a grid of cells).
5
Topology records connectivity and adjacency — the structure spaghetti data lacked.
6
Vector and raster each have trade-offs; resolution and structure drive precision, storage, and analysis.

Wrap-up: an ungraded check-your-understanding quiz is posted in Canvas.
